kjv_text: I am poured out like water, and all my bones are out of joint... I may tell all my bones: they look and stare upon me.

thread.thread: David described the body of a dying man -- bones out of joint from hanging by the arms, every bone countable and exposed, onlookers staring (Ps 22:14,17) -- so exactly it reads like an eyewitness account of a crucifixion, written about 1000 years before Rome made the cross its instrument. This is a DESCRIPTIVE match (the psalm pictures what crucifixion does to a body), not a verbal NT citation. This is what Jesus bore to bring you home; recorded centuries early so you would know none of it was chance -- He was crushed in your place.
thread.lever: overlooked detail / descriptive match (Ps 22:14,17 pictures what crucifixion does to a body, written centuries before Rome's cross -- descriptive, not a cited verbal fulfilment)
thread.anchor_detail: the dying man's body -- poured out, every joint pulled loose from hanging by the arms, every bone countable and exposed while onlookers stand and stare -- matching crucifixion centuries early
thread.why_fresh: a king described, in the first person, a death by crucifixion he never witnessed and that Rome had not yet adopted as its instrument
thread.gospel_landing: this is what Jesus bore to bring you home -- every wrenched joint, every cold stare, recorded centuries early so you would know none of it was chance; He was crushed in your place; come to Him
thread.rationale: the body-on-the-cross described centuries early as a precise descriptive match, with a grace landing (He was crushed in your place) -- distinct from short #1 (the garments)

beats.text: A king described a dying body so exactly it reads like an eyewitness account of a crucifixion he never saw.

Psalm twenty-two. David wrote it in the first person, but he was describing someone else's death, not his own. Watch the body of the dying man: "I am poured out like water, and all my bones are out of joint…"

Drained — and every joint pulled loose, the way a body hangs when it's suspended by the arms. And: "I may tell all my bones: they look and stare upon me." He could count every bone, stretched and exposed, while onlookers stood and stared at him.
David never saw a crucifixion. Rome would not make it its instrument for centuries. Yet the dying man of his song bears the marks of one — written early.
This is what Jesus bore to bring you home — every wrenched joint, every cold stare, recorded centuries early so you'd know none of it was chance. He was crushed in your place.
